paper:867765810263752934.en_title: Is there a polaron signature in angle-resolved photoemission of CsPbBr3?
paper:867765810263752934.zh_title: 在CsPbBr3的角分辨光电子能谱中是否存在极化子特征？

variables.content: In room-temperature angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on freshly cleaved single crystals of CsPbBr3, the hole effective mass at the valence-band maximum (VBM) located at the bulk Brillouin-zone R point was determined by fitting local quadratic dispersions and found to be m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0}, where m_{0} denotes the free-electron mass; this reported value is the average of four independent measurements on four different cleaved samples that produced individual fitted masses of 0.19\,m_{0}, 0.23\,m_{0}, 0.20\,m_{0}, and 0.19\,m_{0}. The identification of the VBM at the bulk R point was made by photon-energy-dependent ARPES mapping of the perpendicular crystal momentum k_{\perp}, and under the free-electron final-state mapping k_{\perp}=\sqrt{\tfrac{2m_{0}}{\hbar^{2}}\bigl(E_{\mathrm{kin}}\cos^{2}\theta + V_{0}\bigr)} with inner potential parameter V_{0}=5.3\ \mathrm{eV} (where \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, E_{\mathrm{kin}} the photoelectron kinetic energy referenced to the vacuum level, and \theta the electron emission polar angle), the first R point in these measurements corresponds to photon energy h\nu=29\ \mathrm{eV}.

The sequence consisting of (i) ab initio DFPT evaluation of long-wavelength phonons, Born charges, and dielectric response and computation of the long-wavelength Fröhlich vertex and its energy-resolved squared coupling density d(g^{2})/d\\omega to obtain the effective single-mode parameters \\tilde{g}_{\\mathrm{LO}}^{2} and \\hbar\\tilde{\\omega}_{\\mathrm{LO}}, and (ii) a Feynman-polaron calculation using the ab initio bare mass m_{0} and the Fröhlich parameter \\alpha computed from those inputs, reproduces the ARPES-measured hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e for orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ within experimental uncertainty; therefore this ab initio electron–phonon evaluation combined with a Feynman-polaron treatment is a viable predictive method to compute carrier effective masses in lead-halide perovskites when the Fröhlich mechanism dominates carrier dressing.

Polaron‑parameter calculations performed with variational polaron theory and material dielectric and effective‑mass inputs yield an estimated polaron radius r_p ≈ 36 Å for CsPbBr3; using the approximate mapping between polaron spatial extent and the carrier‑density threshold for overlap n_{\mathrm{overlap}}\sim 1/r_p^3 (modulo order‑unity packing factors), the larger r_p in CsPbBr3 reduces n_{\mathrm{overlap}} and thereby increases the propensity for spatial overlap of distinct polarons and for emitted optical phonons from different polarons to be reabsorbed, which enhances the density‑dependent hot‑phonon bottleneck observed experimentally in CsPbBr3.

The experimental hole effective mass m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0} and the orthorhombic one-shot G_{0}W_{0} quasiparticle hole mass m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} agree within the stated experimental uncertainty and the acknowledged computational caveats (such as partial Wannier interpolation and method sensitivity); therefore the ARPES-observed effective mass can be accounted for by electron–electron correlation effects already included in the one-shot G_{0}W_{0} quasiparticle description and does not require invoking an additional electron–phonon (Fröhlich-type large-polaron) mass renormalization to explain the measured value.

The momentum-resolved valence-band dispersion of single-crystalline orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ at room temperature was measured by angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy (ARPES); here the measured quasiparticle energy as a function of the in-plane crystal momentum was fitted near the valence-band maximum (VBM) by the isotropic in-plane parabolic dispersion E(k)=E_{\\mathrm{VBM}}+\\hbar^{2}k^{2}/(2m_{\\mathrm{exp}}), where k is the magnitude of the two-dimensional in-plane wave vector (units Å^{-1}), \\hbar is the reduced Planck constant, and m_e is the free electron mass, and the energy zero was set at the VBM defined as the energy of the maximum of the energy-distribution curve (EDC) at the band top; this fit, performed on the upper valence band along the surface high-symmetry direction labeled \\bar{\\Gamma}-\\bar{M}, returns an experimental hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e.

A Fröhlich-type large-polaron mass renormalization of approximately 50% applied to the orthorhombic one-shot G_{0}W_{0} bare-band hole mass m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} would increase the mass to about 0.339\,m_{0}; given the experimentally measured hole mass 0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0} reported here, such a 50% enhancement would exceed the experimental value by roughly 0.136\,m_{0}, which corresponds to more than eight times the quoted experimental standard deviation, and therefore a 50% polaronic renormalization of that magnitude would be readily detectable as a statistically significant discrepancy between the ARPES measurement and the orthorhombic G_{0}W_{0} bare-band reference under the present uncertainty assumptions.

For the photoexcited carriers in $CsPbBr_{3}$ nanocrystal thin films, the carrier effective mass renormalization due to polaron formation is characterized by a polaron mass $m_{p}$ equal to $3.5$ times the bare band effective mass $m^{*}$, with the bare mass taken as $m^{*}=0.21\,m_{e}$ where $m_{e}$ is the free-electron rest mass ($m_{e}=9.10938356\times10^{-31}\,$kg); explicitly, $m_{p}=3.5\cdot 0.21\cdot m_{e}$. This multiplicative factor is adopted from prior theoretical and experimental studies of related lead-halide perovskites and is assumed to be applicable under the present sample morphology, temperature, and excitation conditions.
      See cited literature providing polaron mass estimates used by the authors.

Applying the Feynman variational polaron-model weak-to-intermediate coupling expansion
      m_{\\mathrm{pol}}=m_{0}\\left(1+\\frac{\\alpha}{6}+0.025\\alpha^{2}+\\cdots\\right),
      with the inputs m_{0}=0.17\\,m_e and \\alpha=1.81 produces a polaron-renormalized hole mass m_{\\mathrm{pol}}=0.24\\,m_e; this theoretical mass renormalization reduces the discrepancy between the computed bare mass and the ARPES-derived mass to within the experimental uncertainty of \\pm0.02\\,m_e.

Because the electronic photohole created instantaneously by an ARPES probe initially experiences an unrelaxed lattice and the lattice relaxation to form a polaron occurs on a slower timescale set by the phonon frequency $\omega_{0}$, ARPES can probe an initially more itinerant-hole dispersion (producing broad dispersive spectral intensity) prior to the lattice-localized heavy-polaron state with exponentially reduced coherent bandwidth, implying that ARPES spectra can simultaneously exhibit broad momentum-resolved dispersion and polaron-induced incoherent sidebands.
      V. Perebeinos and P. B. Allen, Phys. Rev. Lett. 85 (2000) 5178.

ARPES single-particle spectral-function measurements at finite experimental energy resolution produce a quasiparticle peak whose linewidth is determined by combined instrumental broadening, thermal disorder, and intrinsic many-body scattering; in CsPbBr$_3$ the relevant optical longitudinal-optical (LO) phonon energies are \\leq25\\ \\mathrm{meV} and phonon-replica satellites separated by these energies cannot be resolved because the experimental quasiparticle linewidth exceeds the mode energy separations, yet the ARPES-measured quasiparticle dispersion and the fitted increase in effective mass still faithfully reflect the quasiparticle mass renormalization due to electron–phonon interactions, so that extraction of an increased effective mass from the fitted dispersion constitutes a valid signature of polaronic mass dressing even in the absence of resolved replica peaks.
